narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to an average of around the 6th decile for those who passed—pushing the prediction firmly toward expecting failure. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level versus the typical 6th decile for successful bar takers, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, signaling early academic struggles that often carry over. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 42 stood out positively, well above the 37 average for passers, and it worked against the failure prediction as the third-most influential element. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 was a bit below the 3.24 average for those who passed, and your upper-middle family income aligned with many successful students, these played smaller roles here. Being full-time is common among bar passers, so that fit the norm without much sway. Overall, your profile compared to typical accepted students—who often enter with mid-to-high school deciles and solid early grades—showed gaps in those sustained academic rankings that outweighed your strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, just the model's read on bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work to lift your GPA closer to the passing average. You could also explore part-time options or prep courses proven to help students in similar starting positions succeed on the bar. Many have turned around predictions like this with focused effort—reapplying after addressing these key areas could change the outcome next time. Keep pushing; your strong LSAT shows you have real aptitude to build on.
